title: Civil servants’ remuneration in Berlin
text:
1. In principle, only a legal provision that is decisive for the underlying proceedings can be the subject of specific judicial review pursuant to Article 100.1 of the Basic Law. Nevertheless, the conciliatory function of judicial review proceedings permits the extension of the subject matter and time period under review beyond the matter referred in the proceedings if this is required to guarantee effective legal protection and it is expected that the Federal Constitutional Court will receive a considerable number of referrals concerning comparable provisions in view of its exclusive power to declare laws void.

2. The principle of alimentation (Article 33.5 of the Basic Law) requires the state to grant civil servants and their families a lifelong income commensurate with their office. In combination with the principle of life tenure, its purpose is to ensure civil servants’ independence in the interest of an effective and impartial administration in line with rule of law principles. The civil service system thus also protects the principle of liberal democracy from encroachments.

3. The guarantee of remuneration commensurate with a civil servant’s office laid down in Article 33.5 of the Basic Law constitutes a constitutional directive that binds the legislator deciding on remuneration; the legislator has broad leeway for the specific implementation of this directive. The legislator has exceeded the limits of this leeway when remuneration is evidently inadequate in view of the purpose and substance of the principle of alimentation. This is subject to review by the Federal Constitutional Court.

4. According to the case-law of the European Court of Human Rights, constitutional review must guarantee that civil servants, who are not allowed to strike, can rely on judicial protection as an effective means for asserting their individual constitutional right to an appropriate level of income.

5. Judicial review as to whether remuneration is evidently inadequate and therefore violates Article 33.5 of the Basic Law involves three steps: In a first step, compliance with the requirement of minimum remuneration is assessed, insofar as this is necessary (preliminary assessment). In a second step, it is assessed whether the development of the level of remuneration reflects the general economic and financial circumstances and the general standard of living over the years (adjustment assessment). Finally, if a violation of the principle of alimentation is found in the preliminary assessment or the adjustment assessment, it is reviewed in a third step whether this violation may be justified under constitutional law.

6. Insofar as the legislator has margins of appreciation with regard to the determination, assessment and attribution of the aspects relevant to alimentation to be considered here, these margins give rise to a limited scope of review by the Federal Constitutional Court, which is restricted to an examination of the plausibility and tenability of the legislator’s assessments. The legislative margins correspond to a substantive burden of proof, which – insofar as it has not been met in the legislative process – can be satisfied ex post in the court proceedings by the respective employer that is informed of the relevant considerations. This replaces the compliance with procedural requirements that was necessary according to the previous case-law of the Federal Constitutional Court.

7. Civil servants can only be free from existential financial worries, which safeguards their independence, if their remuneration is set at a level that guarantees sufficient protection from a real risk of poverty for them and their families. This is only the case if their income amounts to at least 80% of the median equivalised income (requirement of minimum remuneration), which is the threshold below which people face economic precarity. This constitutes a further development of the Court’s previous assessment based on the level of social welfare assistance. If remuneration falls short of the minimum level, this is sufficient for finding a violation of the principle of alimentation; no adjustment assessment is then necessary.

8. Whether the development of the level of remuneration has sufficiently reflected the general economic and financial circumstances and the general standard of living over the years must be reviewed in the context of a two-step assessment on the basis of several criteria that can be derived from the principle of alimentation (adjustment assessment).

a) In the first step of the assessment, the development of the level of remuneration is compared with three economic parameters (the development of collectively bargained wages in the public sector, of nominal wages and of consumer prices); moreover, the different remuneration grades within the system are compared and the gaps between the individual remuneration grades considered. The development of the level of remuneration and the development of the economic parameters is assessed on the basis of an index that uses 1996 as the base year. A significant deviation of at least 5% of the level of remuneration from one of these benchmarks indicates evident disregard for the principle of alimentation (first, second and third parameter). Both a significant narrowing of the gaps between remuneration grades (direct violation) and failure to reach the required minimum level of remuneration in a lower remuneration grade (indirect violation) can violate the requirement of distinction between different remuneration grades.

b) In a second step, the results of the first step must be combined with further criteria relevant to alimentation in an overall assessment. If there is a significant deviation regarding at least two parameters, unconstitutional underpayment must be presumed. If there is no deviation, it is presumed that remuneration is commensurate with civil servants’ office. If there is deviation regarding one parameter, the results of the first step of the assessment must be reviewed in particular detail in the second step. Presumptions established in the first step of the assessment can either be confirmed or rebutted.

I. Several civil servants employed by the Land Berlin challenged their remuneration in the courts. The question of whether the remuneration paid to the plaintiffs in their respective remuneration grades in the relevant years was compatible with the constitutional principle of alimentation (Article 33.5 of the Basic Law) was referred to the Federal Constitutional Court in specific judicial review proceedings.

II. The Second Panel of the Federal Constitutional Court extended its review beyond the respective remuneration grades of the plaintiffs in the initial proceedings to all remuneration grades within Remuneration Bracket A in the 2008-2020 period in order to settle the matter to the greatest possible extent, given that there are many further pending proceedings in this matter.

The Court held that approximately 95% of the remuneration grades under review were incompatible with the principle of alimentation and therefore unconstitutional in the 2008-2020 period. The legislator of the Land Berlin must enact a new statutory framework that is compatible with the Constitution by 31 March 2027.

Federal Constitutional Court:

- 2 BvL 6/17, 04.05.2020, Entscheidungen des Bundesverfassungsgerichts – BVerfGE 155, 77, ECLI:DE:BVerfG:2020:ls20200504.2bvl000617;
- 2 BvL 2/17, 16.10.2018, BVerfGE 149, 382, ECLI:DE:BVerfG:2018:ls20181016.2bvl000217, [GER-2018-3-025], press release available in English on the Court’s website; 
- 2 BvR 1738/12, 2 BvR 1395/13, 2 BvR 1068/14, 2 BvR 646/15, 12.06.2018, BVerfGE 148, 296, ECLI:DE:BVerfG:2018:rs20180612.2bvr173812, [GER-2018-2-017], decision available in English on the Court’s website;
- 2 BvL 19/09, 2 BvL 20/09, 2 BvL 5/13, 2 BvL 20/14, 17.11.2015, BVerfGE 140, 240, ECLI:DE:BVerfG:2015:ls20151117.2bvl001909;
- 2 BvL 17/09, 2 BvL 18/09, 2 BvL 3/12, 2 BvL 4/12, 2 BvL 5/12, 2 BvL 6/12, 2 BvL 1/14, 05.05.2015, BVerfGE 139, 64, ECLI:DE:BVerfG:2015:ls20150505.2bvl001709, [GER-2015-2-008], decision available in English on the Court’s website.

European Court of Human Rights:

- 59433/18, 59477/18, 59481/18, 59494/18, 14.12.2023, Humpert and Others v. Germany, ECLI:CE:ECHR:2023:1214JUD005943318.
